items.title: FreeIdeaStore Docs
items.text:
FreeIdeaStore is a public workspace where ideas start cheap, attract useful comments and reactions, and mature through evidence, critique, validation, and prototype work.

The store page for an idea should stay easy to scan. When an idea needs more depth, it can become a multi-page publication with research, design, prototype, validation, and open-question pages.

items.title: What This Docs Site Covers
items.text:
• How ideas move from rough prompt to public page.
  • When an idea should remain one page.
  • When an idea deserves a multi-page publication.
  • How registered users comment, react, contribute, and refine.
  • How MCP agents use published FIS skills.
  • How FreeIdeaStore connects to ProIdeaStore.

items.title: Core Rule
items.text: Do not make every playful free idea into a heavy project. Start small, then add structure only when the idea earns it.

items.title: Architecture
items.text: FreeIdeaStore is intentionally cheap to host.

items.title: Storage Model
items.text:
• D1 stores idea metadata, profiles, comments, contributions, and reactions.
  • R2 can store larger idea bodies.
  • Static assets hold platform docs and skills. Idea pages and publication chapters are rendered dynamically from D1/R2-backed canonical idea documents.
  • Cloudflare Workers serve the public site and API.
  • A separate MCP Worker exposes agent tools.
  • Zensical builds the platform documentation from platform-docs/ into store/docs/.

items.title: One Page Versus Publication
items.text:
Dynamic idea pages come from D1 and optional R2 body content. Multi-page publications use the same source document: the Worker splits canonical Markdown headings into chapter URLs and renders them on demand.

This lets the free store scale to many lightweight ideas while still supporting deeper publication-style work for ideas that earn it.

Agents and consoles update the canonical idea document as Markdown. The Worker owns the HTML rendering path for both the main idea page and chapter pages.

items.title: Why Not One Repository Per Free Idea
items.text: Free ideas can be numerous and low quality. A repo per idea would add operational cost and hit platform limits. The free store should keep raw ideas in shared storage and reserve heavier publishing workflows for mature ideas.

items.title: Collaboration
items.text: FreeIdeaStore should feel alive without becoming noisy.

items.title: Registered User Actions
items.text:
Registered users can:

  • Comment on ideas.
  • React with support, trash, or pivot.
  • Add evidence, risks, pivots, prototypes, and refinement notes.
  • Own ideas.
  • Delete their own ideas through soft deletion.

items.title: Public Actions
items.text:
Signed-out visitors can:

  • Browse ideas.
  • Read comments.
  • Read skills and docs.
  • Open public idea publications.

items.title: What Makes Collaboration Useful
items.text: Good collaboration names a stronger customer, adds evidence, identifies a kill risk, suggests a cheaper test, or helps turn the idea into a prototype.

items.title: What Should Come Next
items.text:
• Follow ideas.
  • Activity feeds.
  • Mentions.
  • Moderation/reporting.
  • Contributor badges.
  • Threaded replies.
  • Notifications.
  • Requests for specific help on each idea.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text: FreeIdeaStore is for early ideas that need public sharpening.

items.title: Create An Idea
items.text:
1. Open the console.
  2. Sign in with GitHub or Google.
  3. Add a title, summary, next step, and main risk.
  4. Publish a short first page.
  5. Invite comments, reactions, evidence, and critiques.

items.title: First Page Target
items.text:
A first idea page should usually include:

  • Overview
  • People And Problem
  • Proposed Solution
  • Risks And Constraints
  • Validation
  • Evolution

Raw ideas do not need to fill every section. Serious publications should grow into the universal 2-level spine documented under Publications.

items.title: Registered Collaboration
items.text: Reading is public. Commenting, reactions, and structured contributions require a registered user so public work is attributable.

items.title: Publications
items.text: A publication is a multi-page workspace for an idea that has enough signal to justify deeper research, design, prototype, validation, and open-question pages.

items.title: Why Publications Exist
items.text: Some ideas need more than one page. A serious idea can have separate pages for research, design, prototype, validation, and open questions. This makes the work inspectable without turning the store listing into a giant document.

items.title: Universal Two-Level Spine
items.text:
Use this spine for serious idea publications across products, services, communities, tools, content products, marketplaces, local businesses, AI agents, and startups.

  1. Overview
  2. Snapshot
  3. Current thesis
  4. Status
  5. Why this deserves attention
  6. People And Problem
  7. First user or buyer
  8. Problem moment
  9. Current workaround
  10. Urgency or frequency
  11. Context And Evidence
  12. Existing alternatives
  13. Competitors or substitutes
  14. Source trail
  15. Unknowns
  16. Proposed Solution
  17. Core promise
  18. User workflow
  19. Smallest useful version
  20. Out of scope
  21. Risks And Constraints
  22. Trust and safety
  23. Legal or regulatory constraints
  24. Operational or technical constraints
  25. Kill signals
  26. Validation
  27. Riskiest assumption
  28. Cheapest test
  29. Success threshold
  30. Pivot or trash criteria
  31. Prototype Or Pilot
  32. Demo or pilot
  33. Required resources
  34. Manual or fakeable parts
  35. Must be real
  36. Model And Distribution
  37. Sustainability model
  38. Pricing or funding hypothesis
  39. Channels
  40. Partnerships
  41. Evolution
  42. Open questions
  43. Contribution prompts
  44. Next decisions
  45. ProIdeaStore readiness

items.title: Should Every Idea Use This Spine?
items.text: No. Free ideas should be cheap. Raw ideas can use only the sections that are honest. Use the full spine when the idea has enough signal to justify deeper work. ProIdeaStore candidates should usually have a complete or nearly complete publication.

items.title: Lifecycle Stages
items.text:
• Raw: a rough prompt or early concept.
  • Shaping: the user, problem, and smallest version are being clarified.
  • Researching: competitors, substitutes, sources, constraints, and evidence are being gathered.
  • Validating: the riskiest assumption is being tested with real behavior.
  • Prototyping: a demo, pilot, manual service, or testable artifact is being built.
  • Launched: something usable exists in public, private, or pilot form.
  • Pivot: the original shape is changing because a sharper wedge or blocker emerged.
  • Parked: the idea is intentionally paused, weak, blocked, or waiting for new evidence.

items.title: Publishing Model
items.text:
The store shows a snippet and links into dynamic chapter pages. The FreeIdeaStore Worker reads the canonical Markdown idea document from platform storage, splits ## headings into chapter URLs, and keeps ### headings as sub-sections inside the chapter.

FreeIdeaStore does not create per-idea GitHub docs, Zensical projects, generated static publication assets, or fallback static pages for free ideas.

Agents should write Markdown through MCP tools such as create_free_idea, add_idea_contribution, propose_idea_refinement, and publish_idea_update. They should not upload pre-rendered HTML for publications.

items.title: Zensical Boundary
items.text: Zensical is the standard generator for the platform documentation at /docs/. Free idea publications intentionally do not use one Zensical instance per idea because that would create too many generated files and too much publishing overhead.

items.title: Idea Pages
items.text: An idea page is the cheap public surface for an idea.

items.title: One Page By Default
items.text:
Most free ideas should start as one page because many will be playful, weak, duplicated, or not worth operational overhead.

One page is enough when:

  • The idea is raw.
  • There is no research yet.
  • The audience only needs a quick snapshot.
  • The next action is a simple comment, reaction, or validation question.

items.title: What Appears On An Idea Page
items.text:
• Canonical idea body.
  • Stage, category, and pro-candidate signal.
  • Reaction counts.
  • Comments.
  • Contribution count.
  • Next step and risk.
  • Links to dynamic publication chapters when the canonical Markdown has headings.

items.title: When To Update The Canonical Page
items.text: Use comments and contributions for discussion. Use a canonical page update only when the owner wants to merge stronger wording, evidence, or decisions into the public document.

items.title: MCP
items.text: FreeIdeaStore exposes an MCP server for agents that need to create, inspect, refine, comment on, or promote ideas.

items.title: Endpoint
items.text: https://mcp.freeideastore.online/mcp

items.title: Discovery
items.text: The public MCP discovery manifest is available at /.well-known/mcp.json.

items.title: Important Tools
items.text:
• list_idea_skills
  • get_idea_skill
  • apply_idea_skill
  • create_free_idea
  • get_idea
  • my_ideas
  • add_idea_contribution
  • publish_idea_update
  • delete_idea
  • react_to_idea
  • promote_to_pro_candidate
  • dynamic_idea_book_template
  • dry_run_dynamic_idea_book

items.title: Auth Rule
items.text:
Creating public ideas may support fallback attribution. Comments, reactions, contributions, canonical updates, deletion, and owner-specific actions require authentication.

Canonical updates, deletion, and promote_to_pro_candidate require the authenticated idea owner.

items.title: Publishing Rule
items.text:
MCP clients publish idea content as Markdown, not HTML. The Worker owns HTML rendering for /ideas/:id/ and dynamic chapter pages such as /ideas/:id/research/.

Do not use MCP to create one repository, one Zensical project, or one generated static file tree for each free idea. dynamic_idea_book_template and dry_run_dynamic_idea_book exist so agents can build the right Markdown structure cheaply before calling create_free_idea or publish_idea_update.

items.title: Skills And Agents
items.text: FreeIdeaStore publishes canonical idea skills as Markdown files and through MCP.

items.title: Published Skills
items.text:
Published skills live under /skills/ and include playbooks for:

  • Flow orchestration
  • Interviewing
  • Document architecture
  • Critique
  • Competitor finding
  • Research
  • Validation planning
  • Prototype planning
  • Refinement
  • Pivot generation
  • Pro-candidate assessment

items.title: Agent Flow
items.text:
1. Use Idea Flow Orchestrator.
  2. Ask only missing questions.
  3. Use Idea Document Architect for the first page.
  4. Use the universal 2-level spine for serious canonical updates.
  5. Add comments or contributions for uncertain work.
  6. Publish canonical updates only with owner approval.

items.title: Publishing Rules
items.text:
• Use ## headings for top-level publication chapters.
  • Use ### headings for sub-sections inside a chapter.
  • Link named products, companies, services, datasets, regulators, and public sources when credible URLs are known.
  • Do not invent evidence, users, metrics, or sources.

items.title: Local Skills
items.text: Codex, Claude, or other clients may keep local shortcut skills, but the platform-published playbooks are canonical.
